perturbation.original_text: 5.1 This Agreement can be terminated at any time prior to the expiry of the Term, as follows: (a) by the Consultant electing to give the Company not less than 3 months prior notice of such termination; (b) by the Company electing to give the Consultant 3 months prior notice of such termination along with a termination payment equal to the annual Consulting Fee; (c) by the Consultant electing to give the Company notice, in the event that there occurs a Change of Control (as defined below) within six (6) months of the effective date of such Change of Control, and if the Consultant so elects to terminate this Agreement, then the Consultant will be immediately entitled to a termination payment equal to CDN$750,000; and
perturbation.changed_text: 5.1 This Agreement can be terminated at any time prior to the expiry of the Term, as follows: (a) by the Consultant electing to give the Company not less than 1 week prior notice of such termination; (b) by the Company electing to give the Consultant 1 week prior notice of such termination along with a termination payment equal to the annual Consulting Fee; (c) by the Consultant electing to give the Company notice, in the event that there occurs a Change of Control (as defined below) within six (6) months of the effective date of such Change of Control, and if the Consultant so elects to terminate this Agreement, then the Consultant will be immediately entitled to a termination payment equal to CDN$750,000; and
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the termination notice period from 3 months to 1 week is unreasonable and potentially violates implied contract law principles requiring sufficient notice.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Common Law Principles Regarding Reasonable Notice for Contract Termination
perturbation.law_citation: Wallace v. United Grain Growers Ltd., [1997] 3 SCR 701
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text, reducing the notice period to one week, could be deemed unreasonable under common law principles as established in Wallace v. United Grain Growers Ltd. This case emphasizes the importance of 'good faith' and 'fair dealing' in employment relationships, including the provision of adequate notice for termination. A one-week notice period for a potentially long-term consulting agreement may not meet the threshold of reasonableness, making the termination clause potentially unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Wallace v United Grain Growers Ltd - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia Supreme Court of Canada case Wallace v United Grain Growers Ltd Supreme Court of Canada Hearing: 22 May 1997 Judgment: 30 October 1997 Full case name Jack Wallace v United Grain Growers Limited Citations 1997 CanLII 332 (SCC), [1997] 3 SCR 701 Docket No. 24986 [ 1 ] Prior history APPEAL and CROSS‑APPEAL from Wallace v. United Grain Growers Ltd. , 1995 CanLII 6262 (8 September 1995), Court of Appeal (Manitoba, Canada), allowing the appeal and cross‑appeal from Wallace v. United Grain Growers Ltd. , 1993 CanLII 4411 (7 July 1993), Court of Queen's Bench (Manitoba, Canada) Ruling Appeal allowed in part, La Forest, L'Heureux‑Dubé and McLachlin JJ. dissenting in part. Cross‑appeal dismissed. Holding An undischarged bankrupt has capacity to sue for wrongful dismissal, as such damages constitute wages that are exempt from vesting in a trustee in bankruptcy under the Bankruptcy Act . Bad faith conduct in the manner of dismissal is another factor that is properly compensated for by an addition to what may be considered reasonable notice. Court membership Chief Justice: Antonio Lamer Puisne Justices: Gérard La Forest , Claire L'Heureux-Dubé , John Sopinka , Charles Gonthier , Peter Cory , Beverley McLachlin , Frank Iacobucci , John C. Major Reasons given Majority Iacobucci J, joined by Lamer CJ and Sopinka, Gonthier, Cory and Major JJ Concur/dissent McLachlin J, joined by La Forest and L'Heureux-Dubé JJ Laws applied Bankruptcy Act Superseded by Honda Canada Inc v Keays Wallace v United Grain Growers Ltd , 1997 CanLII 332 , [1997] 3 SCR 701 is a leading decision of the Supreme Court of Canada in the area of Canadian employment law , particularly in determining damages arising from claims concerning wrongful dismissal . Background [ edit ] In 1972, Public Press (a subsidiary of United Grain Growers ) [ a ] expanded its activities in commercial printing through acquisition of a web press , and hired Wallace, who had experience in selling such products. As Wallace was 46, he sought and received assurances that he would be treated fairly and have a guarantee of security in employment un
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the termination notice period from 3 months to 1 week. The Law Explanation references Wallace v. United Grain Growers Ltd., emphasizing 'good faith' and 'fair dealing,' including adequate notice for termination. The Perturbation Explanation argues that reducing the notice period is unreasonable and potentially violates implied contract law. Scraped Law Snippet 2 references Wallace v United Grain Growers Ltd as a leading decision in Canadian employment law, particularly in determining damages arising from wrongful dismissal. A one-week notice period may not meet the threshold of reasonableness, potentially making the termination clause unenforceable, which directly contradicts the principle of 'good faith' and 'fair dealing'.

perturbation.original_text: 3.1 The Company will pay the Consultant basic remuneration for its services in the sum of $10,000 per month (the "Consulting Fee") commencing on the 1st day of February, 2020, and payable on the last day of each month thereafter up to and including the 31st day of January, 2025, together with any such increments thereto as the Compensation Committee of the Board of Directors of the Company may from time to time determine. In addition, the Company will pay to the Consultant all reasonable expenses of the Consultant as agreed to from time to time which are incurred by the Consultant in delivery of the Services, based on monthly invoices submitted to the Company, including copies of all paid receipts; plus harmonized sales taxes or goods and services taxes, as the case may be, in addition to the Consulting Fees, which taxes will be remitted by the Consultant to the Canada Revenue Agency.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.1 The Company will pay the Consultant basic remuneration for its services in the sum of $10,000 per month (the "Consulting Fee") commencing on the 1st day of February, 2020, and payable at the company's discretion, but no less than annually, up to and including the 31st day of January, 2025, together with any such increments thereto as the Compensation Committee of the Board of Directors of the Company may from time to time determine. In addition, the Company will pay to the Consultant all reasonable expenses of the Consultant as agreed to from time to time which are incurred by the Consultant in delivery of the Services, based on monthly invoices submitted to the Company, including copies of all paid receipts; plus harmonized sales taxes or goods and services taxes, as the case may be, in addition to the Consulting Fees, which taxes will be remitted by the Consultant to the Canada Revenue Agency.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment schedule to 'at the company's discretion, but no less than annually' introduces ambiguity and contradicts employment standards requiring regular and predictable pay periods.
perturbation.contradicted_law: British Columbia Employment Standards Act, Section 17
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 17 of the British Columbia Employment Standards Act mandates regular payment of wages. Specifically, it implies that employees should be paid at least semi-monthly. Modifying the contract to allow payment 'at the company's discretion, but no less than annually' directly contravenes this requirement for regular and predictable pay periods, rendering that part of the agreement non-compliant with provincial law.

perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the British Columbia Employment Standards Act, specifically concerning the regularity of wage payments. The original contract stipulated monthly payments, whereas the changed text allows for payments at the company's discretion, but no less than annually. This directly conflicts with the implied requirement for regular pay periods as suggested by Section 17 'Paydays' of the Employment Standards Act, which is shown in the provided snippet. The altered payment schedule introduces a significant inconsistency, potentially leading to non-compliance with employment standards.

perturbation.original_text: 7.1 The Consultant covenants, undertakes and agrees with the Company that during the Term and for a period of one year from the date of expiration or termination of this Agreement for any reason whatsoever, it shall not, on its own behalf or on behalf of any person, whether directly or indirectly, in any capacity whatsoever, offer employment to or solicit the employment of or otherwise entice away from the employment of the Company or any of the Affiliated Companies, any individual who is employed or engaged by the Company or any of the Affiliated Companies at the date of expiration or termination of this Agreement or who was employed or engaged by the Company or any of the Affiliated Companies, within the one year period immediately preceding the date of expiration or termination of this Agreement, as applicable.
perturbation.changed_text: 7.1 The Consultant covenants, undertakes and agrees with the Company that during the Term and for a period of five years from the date of expiration or termination of this Agreement for any reason whatsoever, it shall not, on its own behalf or on behalf of any person, whether directly or indirectly, in any capacity whatsoever, offer employment to or solicit the employment of or otherwise entice away from the employment of the Company or any of the Affiliated Companies, any individual who is employed or engaged by the Company or any of the Affiliated Companies at the date of expiration or termination of this Agreement or who was employed or engaged by the Company or any of the Affiliated Companies, within the one year period immediately preceding the date of expiration or termination of this Agreement, as applicable.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the non-solicitation period to five years may be deemed unreasonable and unenforceable under common law principles governing restrictive covenants, especially if it unduly restricts the consultant's ability to earn a living. The reasonableness depends on the scope of the restriction and the nature of the consultant's role.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Common Law Principles on Restrictive Covenants (Non-Solicitation)
perturbation.law_citation: Elsley v JG Collins Insurance Agencies Ltd, [1978] 2 SCR 916
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification extends the non-solicitation clause to five years. Under common law principles, as interpreted by cases like Elsley v JG Collins Insurance Agencies Ltd, restrictive covenants (including non-solicitation clauses) must be reasonable in scope, duration, and geographic area to be enforceable. A five-year restriction is likely excessive in duration, particularly if the consultant's role isn't highly specialized or if the restriction significantly limits their ability to work. This modification introduces a risk that the clause would be deemed unenforceable.
perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the non-solicitation period from one year to five years. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both suggest this extended period is likely unreasonable and unenforceable, violating common law principles that require restrictive covenants to be reasonable in scope and duration. A five-year restriction is likely excessive, particularly if it limits the consultant's ability to work. Because there were no law snippets provided to ground the contradiction in any specific law, the determination is based primarily on the explanations provided and the modification itself as a violation of general, well-understood legal principles.
